title: Tho' The Last Glimpse Of Erin With Sorrow I See.
content:
Tho' the last glimpse of Erin with sorrow I see,
    Yet wherever thou art shall seem Erin to me;
    In exile thy bosom shall still be my home,
    And thine eyes make my climate wherever we room.

    To the gloom of some desert or cold rocky shore,
    Where the eye of the stranger can haunt us no more,
    I will fly with my Coulin, and think the rough wind
    Less rude than the foes we leave frowning behind.

    And I'll gaze on thy gold hair as graceful it wreathes;
    And hang o'er thy soft harp, as wildly it breathes;
    Nor dread that the cold-hearted Saxon will tear
    One chord from that harp, or one lock from that hair.[1]
meaning:
This evocative piece by Thomas Moore, titled "Tho' The Last Glimpse Of Erin With Sorrow I See.", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
